en.extract:
The stork-billed kingfisher (Pelargopsis capensis), is a tree kingfisher which is widely but sparsely distributed in the tropical Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, from India to Indonesia. These kingfishers are large and have a heavy bill. The head is brown and the chin is paler coloured. The sexes are similar in coloration. This is found mostly in streams and ponds in lowland areas with tree cover. This kingfisher is sendentary throughout its range.


Taxonomy

The first formal description of the stork-billed kingfisher was by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in 1766 in the 12th edition of his Systema Naturae. He coined the binomial name Alcedo capensis. Linnaeus based his description on Mathurin Jacques Brisson's "Le martin-pescheur du Cap de Bonne Espérance". Brisson believed his specimen had come from the Cape of Good Hope region of South Africa. The species does not occur in Africa and it was suggested that the specimen had been obtained on the Indonesian island of Java. The specimen is now known to have come from near Chandannagar in West Bengal, India. Linnaeus's specific epithet capensis denotes the Cape of Good Hope. The current genus Pelargopsis was introduced by the German zoologist Constantin Gloger in 1841.
Thirteen subspecies are recognised:

P. c. capensis (Linnaeus, 1766) – Nepal through India to Sri Lanka
P. c. osmastoni (Baker, ECS, 1934) – Andaman Islands
P. c. intermedia Hume, 1874 – Nicobar Islands
P. c. burmanica Sharpe, 1870 – Myanmar to Indochina and south to north Malay Peninsula
P. c. malaccensis Sharpe, 1870 – central and south Malay Peninsula, Riau Archipelago and Lingga Islands
P. c. cyanopteryx (Oberholser, 1909) – Sumatra, Bangka Island and Belitung Island
P. c. simalurensis Richmond, 1903 – Simeulue Island  (off the west coast of the island of Sumatra in Indonesia)
P. c. sodalis Richmond, 1903 – Banyak, Nias, Batu and Mentawai Islands (off the west coast of Sumatra)
P. c. innominata (van Oort, 1910) – Borneo
P. c. javana (Boddaert, 1783) – Java
P. c. floresiana Sharpe, 1870 – Bali to Flores  (Lesser Sunda Islands)
P. c. gouldi Sharpe, 1870 – north Philippines
P. c. gigantea Walden, 1874 – central and south Philippines
The insular forms nesoeca on the Nias and Batu Islands as well as isoptera on Mentawai Island are here subsumed within sodalis. Prior to the change of type locality to Chandannagar, the birds in India were placed in the subspecies gurial but this race is now synonymized with the nominate race capensis.


Description
It is a very large kingfisher, measuring 35 cm (14 in) in length. The adult has a green back, blue wings and tail, and olive-brown head. Its underparts and neck are buff. The very large bill and legs are bright red. The flight of the stork-billed kingfisher is laboured and flapping, but direct. Sexes are similar. There are 13 races or subspecies, differing mostly in plumage detail, but P. c. gigantea of the Sulu Archipelago in the Philippines has a white head, neck and underparts. 


Habits
The stork-billed kingfisher lives in a variety of well-wooded habitats near lakes, rivers, or coasts. It perches quietly whilst seeking food, and is often inconspicuous despite its size. It is territorial and will chase away eagles and other large predators. This species hunts fish, frogs, crabs, rodents and young birds. They were also recorded feeding on fruits.
They are largely sedentary but capable of dispersal and may move in search of water during dry weather.
Adults dig their nests in river banks, decaying trees, or tree termite nests. Both members of the pair take part in next excavation. They initially strike the soil in flight to loosen soil and after creating a perch they cling and peck to excavate the hole. A clutch of two to five round white eggs is typical. The eggs are about 34 to 39 mm long and about 29 to 32 mm wide.
The call of this noisy kingfisher is a low and far reaching peer-por-por repeated about every 5 seconds, as well cackling ke-ke-ke-ke-ke-ke.


Gallery


References


External links

[[File:PelargopsisBurmanica.jpg|thumb|right|''Pelargopsis capensis burmanica'' by [[John Gerrard Keulemans|Keulemans]]]]
The '''stork-billed kingfisher''' ('''''Pelargopsis capensis'''''), is a [[tree kingfisher]] which is widely but sparsely distributed in the tropical [[Indian subcontinent]] and [[Southeast Asia]], from [[India]] to [[Indonesia]]. These kingfishers are large and have a heavy bill. The head is brown and the chin is paler coloured. The sexes are similar in coloration. This is found mostly in streams and ponds in lowland areas with tree cover. This [[kingfisher]] is sendentary throughout its range.

==Taxonomy==
[[File: Stork Billed Kingfisher.jpg|left|thumb|''P. c. capensis'' at [[Ranthambore National Park]]]]
The first [[Species description|formal description]] of the stork-billed kingfisher was by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in 1766 in the [[12th edition of Systema Naturae|12th edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]''. He coined the [[binomial name]] ''Alcedo capensis''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1766 | title=Systema naturae: per regna tria natura, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=12th | volume=1, Part 1 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii  | place=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=la | page=180 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/42946376 }}</ref> Linnaeus based his description on [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]]'s "Le martin-pescheur du Cap de Bonne Espérance". Brisson believed his specimen had come from the [[Cape of Good Hope]] region of South Africa.<ref>{{cite book | last=Brisson | first=Mathurin Jacques | author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson | year=1760 | title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode contenant la division des oiseaux en ordres, sections, genres, especes & leurs variétés: Supplement d'Ornithologie | volume=4| page=488, Plate 36 Fig. 3 | place=Paris | publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/36195675 }}</ref> The species does not occur in Africa and it was suggested that the specimen had been obtained on the Indonesian island of [[Java]].<ref name=sharpe>{{cite journal | last=Sharpe  | first=R.B. | author-link=Richard Bowdler Sharpe | year=1870 | title=On the genus ''Pelargopsis'', Gloger | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | pages=61–69 [66] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/28554866 }}</ref><ref name=oberholser>{{cite journal | last=Oberholser | first=Harry C. | author-link=Harry C. Oberholser | year=1909 | title=Revision of the kingfisher genus ''Ramphalcyon'' (''Pelargopsis'') | journal=Proceedings of the United States National Museum | volume=35 | issue=1657 | pages=657–680 [663–665] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/7495562 | doi=10.5479/si.00963801.1657.657}}<!--also from https://archive.org/stream/biostor-79174#page/n0/mode/1up --></ref><ref>{{cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1945 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=5 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=187 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480198 }}</ref> The specimen is now known to have come from near [[Chandannagar]] in West Bengal, India.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Stresemann | first=Erwin | author-link=Erwin Stresemann | year=1952 | title=On the birds collected by Pierre Poivre in Canton, Manila, India and Madagascar (1751–1756) | journal=Ibis | volume=94 | issue=3 | pages=499–523 | doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1952.tb01847.x }}</ref><ref name=hbw>{{cite web | last1=Woodall | first1=P.F. | last2=Kirwan | first2=G.M. | year=2017 | title=Stork-billed Kingfisher (''Pelargopsis capensis'')| editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | editor4-last=Christie | editor4-first=D.A. | editor5-last=de Juana | editor5-first=E. | work=Handbook of the Birds of the World Alive | publisher=Lynx Edicions | url=http://www.hbw.com/node/55744 | access-date=10 June 2017|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Linnaeus's specific epithet ''capensis'' denotes the Cape of Good Hope.<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn= 978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=89–90 }}</ref> The current genus ''Pelargopsis'' was introduced by the German zoologist [[C. L. Gloger|Constantin Gloger]] in 1841.<ref>{{cite book | last=Gloger  | first=Constantin Wilhelm Lambert | author-link=C. L. Gloger | title=Gemeinnütziges Hand- und Hilfsbuch der Naturgeschichte | date=1842 | language=de | place= Breslau | publisher=A. Schulz | page=338 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13515648 }}</ref>

Thirteen subspecies are recognised:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | year=2017 | title=Rollers, ground rollers & kingfishers | work=World Bird List Version 7.2 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/rollers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=28 May 2017 }}</ref>

* ''P. c. capensis'' (Linnaeus, 1766) – Nepal through India to Sri Lanka
* ''P. c. osmastoni'' ([[E. C. Stuart Baker|Baker, ECS]], 1934) – [[Andaman Islands]]
* ''P. c. intermedia'' [[Allan Octavian Hume|Hume]], 1874 – [[Nicobar Islands]]
* ''P. c. burmanica'' [[Richard Bowdler Sharpe|Sharpe]], 1870 – Myanmar to Indochina and south to north [[Malay Peninsula]]
* ''P. c. malaccensis'' Sharpe, 1870 – central and south Malay Peninsula, [[Riau Archipelago]] and [[Lingga Islands]]
* ''P. c. cyanopteryx'' ([[Harry C. Oberholser|Oberholser]], 1909) – [[Sumatra]], [[Bangka Island]] and [[Belitung Island]]
* ''P. c. simalurensis'' [[Charles Wallace Richmond|Richmond]], 1903 – [[Simeulue Island]]  (off the west coast of the island of [[Sumatra]] in Indonesia)
* ''P. c. sodalis'' Richmond, 1903 – [[Banyak Islands|Banyak]], [[Nias]], [[Batu Islands|Batu]] and [[Mentawai Islands Regency|Mentawai Islands]] (off the west coast of Sumatra)
* ''P. c. innominata'' ([[Eduard Daniël van Oort|van Oort]], 1910) – [[Borneo]]
* ''P. c. javana'' ([[Pieter Boddaert|Boddaert]], 1783) – [[Java]]
* ''P. c. floresiana'' Sharpe, 1870 – [[Bali]] to [[Flores]]  ([[Lesser Sunda Islands]])
* ''P. c. gouldi'' Sharpe, 1870 – north Philippines
* ''P. c. gigantea'' [[Arthur Hay, 9th Marquess of Tweeddale|Walden]], 1874 – central and south Philippines
The insular forms ''nesoeca'' on the Nias and Batu Islands as well as ''isoptera'' on Mentawai Island are here subsumed within ''sodalis''. Prior to the change of [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] to Chandannagar, the birds in India were placed in the subspecies ''gurial'' but this race is now [[Synonym (taxonomy)|synonymized]] with the [[nominate race]] ''capensis''.<ref name=hbw/>

==Description==
It is a very large kingfisher, measuring {{cvt|35|cm}} in length.<ref>{{cite book|last=Ali|first=Sálim|year=1996|title=The Book of Indian Birds|edition=12th|publisher=Bombay Natural History Society|location=Bombay|isbn=0-19-563731-3}}</ref> The adult has a green back, blue wings and tail, and olive-brown head. Its underparts and neck are buff. The very large bill and legs are bright red. The flight of the stork-billed kingfisher is laboured and flapping, but direct. Sexes are similar. There are 13 races or [[subspecies]], differing mostly in plumage detail, but ''P. c. gigantea'' of the [[Sulu Archipelago]] in the Philippines has a white head, neck and underparts. 

==Habits==
The stork-billed kingfisher lives in a variety of well-wooded habitats near lakes, rivers, or coasts. It perches quietly whilst seeking food, and is often inconspicuous despite its size. It is territorial and will chase away [[eagle]]s and other large predators. This species hunts fish, frogs, crabs, rodents and young birds.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Wyatt, Sarah |year=1929 |title=The Storkbilled Kingfishser ''Ramphalcyon capensis gurial'' eating birds |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47489885 |journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=33 |issue=3 |page=713}}</ref> They were also recorded feeding on fruits.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=S.S |first=Suresh |title=The Stork-billed Kingfisher Pelargopsis capensis feeding on Caryota urens fruits |url=https://indianbirds.in/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/IB_21_1_Suresh_StorkbilledKingfisher.pdf |journal=Indian Birds |volume=21 |issue=1 |pages=19-20}}</ref>

They are largely sedentary but capable of dispersal and may move in search of water during dry weather.<ref>{{cite journal |author=Begbie, Arundel |year=1906 |title=The Stork-billed Kingfisher (''Pelargopsis gurial'') at Cawnpore. |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/30119416 |journal=J. Bombay Nat. Hist. Soc. |volume=17 |issue=1 |pages=248–249}}</ref>

Adults dig their nests in river banks, decaying trees, or tree termite nests. Both members of the pair take part in next excavation. They initially strike the soil in flight to loosen soil and after creating a perch they cling and peck to excavate the hole. A clutch of two to five round white eggs is typical. The eggs are about 34 to 39 mm long and about 29 to 32 mm wide.<ref>{{cite book |author1=Fry, C Hilary |title=Kingfishers, bee-eaters and rollers. A handbook. |author2=Fry, Kathie |publisher=Christopher Helm |year=1992 |place=London |pages=137–139}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |author=Harrison, C.J.O. |year=1961 |title=Note on some eggs and nests attributed to the Stork- billed Kingfisher, ''Pelargopsis capensis'' (Linne). |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40823482 |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |volume=81 |pages=141–143}}</ref>

The call of this noisy kingfisher is a low and far reaching ''peer-por-por'' repeated about every 5 seconds, as well cackling ''ke-ke-ke-ke-ke-ke''.<ref>{{cite book|last1=Fry|first1=C.Hilary|last2=Fry|first2=Kathie|last3=Harris|first3=Alan|title=Kingfishers, Bee-eaters, and Rollers|date=2000|publisher=Christopher Helm Publishers|location=London, Eng.|isbn=978-0-7136-5206-2|pages=137–139|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hFSdA6xYqRMC|access-date=11 April 2016}}</ref>

zh.extract:
鹳嘴翡翠（学名：Pelargopsis capensis）为翠鸟科鹳嘴翡翠属的鸟类。该物种的模式产地在非洲好望角。其种加词“capensis”意为“南非开普省的/好望角的”。


保护
中国国家重点保护动物等级：二级


亚种
鹳嘴翡翠云南亚种（学名：Pelargopsis capensis burmanica）。在中国大陆，分布于云南等地。该物种的模式产地在缅甸。


参考文献
